data.title: Is Lumentum’s Recent 90% Rally Warranted After Raised Guidance for 2025?
data.description:
If you have Lumentum Holdings on your radar, you are definitely not alone. It is one of those stocks that seems to keep investors talking, whether they are asking if it is still worth holding after a spectacular run or wondering if now is the right moment to jump in. Over the past year, the stock has soared by 134.5%, with an eye-catching 90.7% gain just since the start of this year. Even the three- and five-year returns have outperformed many peers, climbing 132.1% and 91.4%, respectively.

That being said, this ride has not always been smooth. While last week saw a solid 2.2% uptick, the stock is down by 4.5% over the past month, a reminder that market sentiment and sector trends continue to sway short-term results. Some of this optimism has been linked to broader market strength in technology and communications, but rising interest rates and shifting competitive dynamics have also played a role in shaping investor expectations.

Of course, the big question is whether the current price, now hovering at $163.23 at last close, reflects Lumentum’s true worth. Valuation is where things get interesting. Based on a straightforward scorecard measuring six different checks of undervaluation, Lumentum comes in with a value score of just 0. According to these standard methods, the company does not appear undervalued at all. But before you decide what that means for your portfolio, it is helpful to take a look at what each of these valuation methods actually says and then, at the end, explore a more nuanced approach to gauging true value.

Lumentum Holdings scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Lumentum Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model is one of the most widely used methods for estimating a company's intrinsic value. It works by forecasting the company's expected future cash flows and then discounting them back to today's value using a required rate of return. In simple terms, it attempts to answer what those cash flows are worth in today's dollars.

For Lumentum Holdings, the current free cash flow stands at -$43.37 million, which means the company has recently spent more cash than it generated. However, projections indicate a more optimistic outlook. Analyst estimates cover the next five years and, for Lumentum, future free cash flow is expected to steadily grow, reaching around $534.33 million by 2028. Extending the timeline further using conservative growth assumptions, the DCF model incorporates these longer-term projections to estimate the company’s total value.

According to this model, Lumentum’s intrinsic fair value is $143.37 per share. Compared to the current trading price of $163.23, the DCF suggests the stock is about 13.9% overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: Lumentum Holdings Price vs Sales

The price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is a preferred valuation method for companies like Lumentum Holdings in the growth stage or those with volatile earnings, as it focuses on overall revenue instead of profits, which may fluctuate year to year. For profitable and established businesses, multiples like the P/S help investors assess whether the stock price makes sense relative to the company's top-line performance.

Normally, growth prospects and industry risk play a big role in determining what a “fair” P/S multiple should be. Companies expected to post higher revenue growth or possessing lower risk profiles often justify higher multiples, while slower-growing or riskier companies tend to receive lower ones.

Lumentum Holdings currently trades at a P/S ratio of 7.04x. For context, that is significantly above the industry average of 2.22x and the average among its peers, which sits at 4.55x. At first glance, this might suggest some level of overvaluation compared to the broader sector.

This is where Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” comes in. Unlike simple peer or industry comparisons, the Fair Ratio incorporates a range of factors such as Lumentum’s expected growth, risk, profit margin, industry trends, and company size. In this case, the Fair Ratio for Lumentum is calculated at 4.08x, giving a much more customized benchmark for what is “fair” for the stock given its unique characteristics.

Comparing the current P/S of 7.04x to the Fair Ratio of 4.08x, Lumentum trades well above what would be considered reasonable after adjusting for its fundamentals and outlook. This suggests the market may be pricing in more optimism than is warranted by the company’s underlying story.

Result: OVERVALUED

A Narrative is a clear, evidence-backed story about a company’s future, created by connecting your own view of how the business will perform with specific assumptions about its future revenue, earnings, and margins, ultimately calculating a fair value. Narratives bridge the gap between “just the numbers” and real-world context, letting investors see not just what a company is worth, but why.

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates the intrinsic value of a company by forecasting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. This approach helps investors understand what the business is worth based on its ability to generate cash in the future.

For Christian Dior, the DCF analysis uses a two-stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. data.title: Investors Should Be Encouraged By China Hongqiao Group's (HKG:1378) Returns On Capital
data.description:
What trends should we look for it we want to identify stocks that can multiply in value over the long term? Ideally, a business will show two trends; firstly a growing return on capital employed (ROCE) and secondly, an increasing amount of capital employed. If you see this, it typically means it's a company with a great business model and plenty of profitable reinvestment opportunities. With that in mind, the ROCE of China Hongqiao Group (HKG:1378) looks great, so lets see what the trend can tell us.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

What Is Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)?

Just to clarify if you're unsure, ROCE is a metric for evaluating how much pre-tax income (in percentage terms) a company earns on the capital invested in its business. The formula for this calculation on China Hongqiao Group is:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.26 = CN¥39b ÷ (CN¥228b - CN¥80b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to June 2025).

Therefore, China Hongqiao Group has an ROCE of 26%. In absolute terms that's a great return and it's even better than the Metals and Mining industry average of 10%.

View our latest analysis for China Hongqiao Group

In the above chart we have measured China Hongqiao Group's prior ROCE against its prior performance, but the future is arguably more important. If you're interested, you can view the analysts predictions in our free analyst report for China Hongqiao Group .

So How Is China Hongqiao Group's ROCE Trending?

The trends we've noticed at China Hongqiao Group are quite reassuring. The numbers show that in the last five years, the returns generated on capital employed have grown considerably to 26%. The amount of capital employed has increased too, by 56%. The increasing returns on a growing amount of capital is common amongst multi-baggers and that's why we're impressed.

In another part of our analysis, we noticed that the company's ratio of current liabilities to total assets decreased to 35%, which broadly means the business is relying less on its suppliers or short-term creditors to fund its operations. So this improvement in ROCE has come from the business' underlying economics, which is great to see.

In Conclusion...

All in all, it's terrific to see that China Hongqiao Group is reaping the rewards from prior investments and is growing its capital base. Since the stock has returned a staggering 618% to shareholders over the last five years, it looks like investors are recognizing these changes. With that being said, we still think the promising fundamentals mean the company deserves some further due diligence.
